text: the room going fuzzy at the corners, and I hold onto the counter instead of you and tell myself I will not have a panic attack. He’s dead. He isn’t here. When I finally look up, you are gone, but the ghost is standing at the top of the stairs, its broken mouth pulled into an obscene smile. Our bedroom door closes, and I am frozen as I watch it turn, its steps sounding down the hallway. I take the stairs one by one and listen for your scream, but the house is quiet. If the ghost of me is in the room with you, it hasn’t revealed itself. No shattered mirrors or flickering lights or sighs. Only you, undressing while it watches and imagining what it would feel like to rake its teeth along your throat, your scent on its blackened tongue. A reminder of the things I, we , so desperately wanted but was the forbidden fruit. But when I throw open the door, there is no ghost leaning over you, and you glance up at me. “If you’re going to keep lecturing me—” you start, and I swallow your words with my mouth on yours. There is no relief in it because the room is heavy with my ghost. From the walls, from the floorboards, it whispers, and I know what it wants. What it’s always wanted. Because I finally have it. Later, after I have claimed every inch of you, you sleep, and I slip out of bed and press myself against the floor. “You can’t have her,” I say, and from inside the house, the ghost of me laughs. We are an ouroboros of desire and degradation. How can I keep it from what we already have? Haven’t I already destroyed you by letting you fall in love with me? Even still, I repeat it over and over, holding to the phrase as if it could ward off evil. For the rest of the night, the ghost keeps away, but I can feel it moving through the house. I don’t sleep. In the morning, you sweep up the salt and collect the candles. “What are you doing?” I ask, and you dump the salt into the trash. “It wasn’t working anyway. Besides, it’s not like it’s hurting us.” You move slowly, as if you are trapped in a dream. “I’m going back to bed,” you say, and you sleep through the afternoon, your phone pinging nonstop with emails and messages. It isn’t like you to ignore work. For hours, I sit beside you watching your chest rise and fall. In the evening, I try to wake you. “Too tired,” you mumble, and I bring you toast and tea clouded with milk. You drink the tea and crumble the toast into bits, and your eyes are hollow as you stare at me. “The house is so lovely. Why did you leave?” “I thought you wanted me to sell it,” I say, and you snuggle down into the blanket. “It’s like living in a book. A Gothic Romance.” You smile, ignoring that such books tend to end in blood. If we are haunted, you’ve evolved into a willing conduit. Your body an altar. An offering not for the dead but for the girl left behind. “You wouldn’t love her if you knew,” I whisper. You tell me you didn’t hear what I said, and I tell you it’s nothing. Not important. I will not let what’s infected this house seduce you. You stay in bed all of the next day, drifting in and out, your body looking insubstantial. You have lost weight, and your skin looks as if you could slip out of it. I want to cry for you, but the tears belong only to my ghost. In this house, they have always been hers. Once night falls, I press my lips to your forehead and close the door behind me. I find your candles. The salt. A knife meant for separating meat from bone. I carry all these things into my old bedroom. The one tucked at the bottom of the stairs, the door all but hidden behind the dresser my father dragged in front of it. The room is as I left it. Spare with its twin bed and single pine dresser. No posters. No pictures. The only ornament the heavy crucifix hanging on the far wall. I could almost laugh with the irony of it. I’m surprised the crucifix hasn’t inverted itself. A haunting growing out of the room my father tried to make holy with his violence. He’d wanted the crucifix as a reminder Jesus was always watching, but all I ever wanted to do was stab myself with it. I pour the salt in a circle and set the candles just outside the perimeter. Squeezing my eyes shut, I pass the knife over my palm, the pain flaring and then dying into a dull ache. The candlelight flickers, casting strange shadows against the wall. I let my blood drop to the floor before stepping inside the circle. I do not turn when I hear the door creak open, do not look when I hear its tongue lapping at my— our —spilled blood, but eventually, it creeps before me on all fours, its head bent low, its tongue still trailing along the floor. “You don’t have to do this. You’ve seen what happens. You leave. You fall in love. You get married,” I say, and I want to scream until my throat is raw. Looking into its face, this younger version of myself, is unnatural. Obscene. I clutch the knife, but there is no comfort in it. No safety. My ghost shifts backward, its eyes gleaming in the candlelight, and shakes its head. Its voice is guttural, each syllable scraping past its teeth. “Give her to me.” “Fuck you.” It laughs. Reaches out a hand and scatters the salt and candles. “Stupid,” it says. The house has always belonged to it. To
